flashcards.front: Why does the rarity of extreme events make them hard to model?
flashcards.back: There are very few historical observations to calibrate from, and some plausible magnitudes of loss may never have occurred in the data, so any assumed distribution carries unavoidable model error — especially since standard distributions are typically fit to the center of the data, not the tail.

flashcards.front: What is the central idea of extreme value theory (EVT)?
flashcards.back: EVT is a branch of applied statistics focused specifically on the tail of a distribution — the extreme outcomes — rather than the central-tendency statistics governed by the central limit theorem. It provides a template for estimating the parameters that describe extreme movements.

flashcards.front: What does the Fisher-Tippett theorem state?
flashcards.back: As the sample size n gets large, the distribution of the maxima M_n (the largest value drawn from each of many large samples) converges to the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution.

flashcards.front: What do μ, σ, and ξ represent in the GEV distribution?
flashcards.back: μ is the location parameter and σ is the scale parameter — related to but distinct from the mean and variance — while ξ is the tail index, which governs the shape (heaviness) of the tail.

flashcards.front: What are the three cases of the GEV distribution based on the sign of ξ?
flashcards.back: ξ > 0 gives the Frechet distribution (heavy tails, like the t-distribution or Pareto); ξ = 0 gives the Gumbel distribution (light tails, like the normal or lognormal); ξ < 0 gives the Weibull distribution (tails lighter than normal, rarely seen in finance).

flashcards.front: What are the three practical ways to decide whether to assume ξ > 0 or ξ = 0?
flashcards.back: (1) Rely on confidence about the parent distribution — e.g., assume ξ > 0 if it's known to be a t-distribution; (2) run a statistical test and use ξ = 0 if it can't be rejected; (3) conservatively assume ξ > 0 to avoid understating tail risk.

flashcards.front: What does the peaks-over-threshold (POT) approach model?
flashcards.back: The conditional distribution of excess losses (X − u) over a high threshold u, given that the threshold has been exceeded — rather than modeling the entire loss distribution.

flashcards.front: What does F_u(x) represent in the POT framework?
flashcards.back: The conditional distribution of the excess over threshold u: F_u(x) = P(X − u ≤ x | X > u), the probability the excess is at most x given that the threshold is exceeded.

flashcards.front: What does the Gnedenko-Pickands-Balkema-deHaan (GPBdH) theorem say?
flashcards.back: As the threshold u gets large, the conditional excess distribution F_u(x) converges to a generalized Pareto (GP) distribution, parameterized by a scale parameter β and a shape parameter ξ (the same ξ as in the GEV distribution).

flashcards.front: What is the tradeoff in choosing the POT threshold u?
flashcards.back: u must be high enough for the GPBdH convergence result to be a good approximation, but low enough that there remain enough exceedances (N_u) to estimate β and ξ reliably via maximum likelihood.

flashcards.front: What is expected shortfall (ES) and why is it a useful complement to VaR?
flashcards.back: ES is the expected loss given that the loss exceeds VaR, i.e., E[L_P | L_P > VaR]. Unlike VaR, it captures the severity of losses beyond the VaR cutoff, giving insight into how bad the tail really is.

flashcards.front: How do GEV and POT differ in parameter count and data efficiency?
flashcards.back: GEV requires one more parameter (location, scale, shape) than POT (scale and shape only). GEV's block-maxima approach can also be less data-efficient, since it keeps only one extreme value per block, whereas POT can use every observation that exceeds the threshold.

flashcards.front: What new concept does multivariate EVT introduce that univariate EVT does not address?
flashcards.back: Tail dependence — the tendency for extreme values in different variables or markets to occur together (e.g., a shock that produces extreme losses in oil markets and in broader financial markets simultaneously).

flashcards.front: Why are elliptical distributions and covariance matrices inadequate for modeling multivariate extremes?
flashcards.back: They describe average co-movement and impose a symmetric dependence structure, but don't capture what happens specifically in the joint tails. Modeling multivariate extremes instead requires copulas — specifically, extreme value (EV) copulas.

flashcards.front: Why does adding dimensions make multivariate EVT much harder?
flashcards.back: Joint extreme events become exponentially rarer as more independent variables are added — e.g., a 1-in-100 univariate event becomes a 1-in-10,000 joint event for two variables and 1-in-1,000,000 for three — drastically shrinking available data while increasing the number of parameters to estimate.

questions.q: According to the Fisher-Tippett theorem, as the sample size n grows large, the distribution of sample maxima M_n converges to which distribution?
choices: The generalized Pareto distribution
The generalized extreme value distribution
The normal distribution
The uniform distribution
questions.explanation: The Fisher-Tippett theorem describes convergence of block maxima to the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution. The generalized Pareto distribution is instead the limiting distribution of POT excesses.

questions.q: In the GEV distribution, a tail index ξ = 0 corresponds to which named distribution, and what tail behavior does it imply?
choices: Frechet distribution; heavy tails
Gumbel distribution; light tails similar to the normal or lognormal distribution
Weibull distribution; tails lighter than the normal distribution
Pareto distribution; heavy tails
questions.explanation: ξ = 0 corresponds to the Gumbel case, which has light tails comparable to the normal or lognormal distribution.

questions.q: A risk manager is confident that portfolio losses follow a Student's t-distribution, which is known to be heavy-tailed. Under the GEV framework, which tail index assumption is most appropriate?
choices: ξ > 0 (Frechet)
ξ = 0 (Gumbel)
ξ < 0 (Weibull)
ξ must equal exactly 1
questions.explanation: If the researcher is confident the parent distribution is heavy-tailed (e.g., a t-distribution), the Frechet case (ξ > 0) is the appropriate assumption.

questions.q: In the POT approach, the function F_u(x) represents:
choices: The unconditional distribution of all losses
The conditional distribution of the excess loss over threshold u, given that the threshold is exceeded
The distribution of block maxima
The probability that the threshold u is never exceeded
questions.explanation: F_u(x) = P(X − u ≤ x | X > u) is the conditional distribution of the excess over the threshold, given the threshold is exceeded.

questions.q: Which of the following is an advantage of the POT approach relative to GEV-based approaches?
choices: POT generally requires fewer estimated parameters
POT does not require choosing a threshold
POT does not share any parameters with GEV
POT can be applied without any assumption about the parent distribution of losses being unknown
questions.explanation: POT generally requires fewer parameters (β and ξ) than GEV-based approaches (μ, σ, and ξ). POT does require choosing a threshold, and both approaches share the tail index ξ.

questions.q: The generalized Pareto distribution's shape relative to the normal distribution near the tail is best described as:
choices: Always below the normal distribution everywhere
Identical to the normal distribution throughout
Dipping slightly below the normal distribution before the tail, then rising above it in the extreme tail, with an approximately linear shape there
Always above the normal distribution everywhere
questions.explanation: The GP distribution dips below the normal distribution just before the tail and then rises above it deep in the tail, giving a roughly linear shape that better matches empirical loss data.

questions.q: A risk analyst estimates GP parameters β = 0.6 and ξ = 0.3, with threshold u = 1.5% and N_u/n = 4%. What is the 1% VaR (in percentage terms)?
Using the same parameters as the previous question (β = 0.6, ξ = 0.3, u = 1.5%, and VaR ≈ 2.53%), what is the corresponding expected shortfall?
Why must the POT threshold u be chosen carefully?
choices: Because u must be high enough for the GP approximation to hold, but low enough to leave sufficient exceedances to estimate the parameters
Because u determines the value of ξ directly through a fixed formula
Because u must always equal the median of the loss distribution
Because choosing u incorrectly changes the sign of β
questions.explanation: The threshold involves a tradeoff: high enough for the GPBdH convergence to be valid, but low enough that enough exceedances remain to estimate β and ξ reliably.

questions.q: Relative to POT, the GEV approach:
choices: Requires estimating one fewer parameter
Requires estimating one additional parameter, and can be less data-efficient because block-maxima methods retain only one extreme value per block
Does not share any parameters with POT
Eliminates the need for a tail index entirely
questions.explanation: GEV requires location, scale, and shape parameters (one more than POT's scale and shape), and block-maxima approaches can discard useful data by keeping only the single largest value per block.

questions.q: Both the GEV and POT approaches share which parameter?
choices: The location parameter μ
The scale parameter β
The tail index ξ
The threshold u
questions.explanation: The tail index ξ has the same interpretation and value in both the GEV and generalized Pareto (POT-based) distributions.

questions.q: The primary reason extreme values are difficult to model is:
choices: They occur too frequently to track accurately
Standard distributions fit to the bulk of the data describe the center well but not necessarily the tail, and extreme events are too rare to provide many calibration observations
Extreme values always follow a normal distribution
Extreme values are not relevant to financial risk management
questions.explanation: Distributions are typically chosen based on how well they fit central-tendency data, which doesn't guarantee a good fit in the tail — and the rarity of extreme events leaves few observations to check this against.

questions.q: What new concept does multivariate EVT introduce that is not present in univariate EVT?
choices: The tail index ξ
Tail dependence — the tendency for extreme values in different variables to occur together
The scale parameter β
The central limit theorem
questions.explanation: Multivariate EVT's central concept is tail dependence: how extreme outcomes in one variable relate to extreme outcomes in others.

questions.q: Why are elliptical distributions and covariance matrices considered inadequate for modeling multivariate extreme values?
choices: They cannot be computed for more than two variables
They describe average co-movement and impose a symmetric dependence structure, which fails to capture tail-specific dependence; extreme value copulas are needed instead
They require too much historical data to be practical
They are only valid for normally distributed single variables and cannot be extended
questions.explanation: Covariance-based and elliptical-distribution approaches summarize average dependence and assume symmetry, but tail dependence is specifically about behavior in the joint extremes, which requires extreme value (EV) copulas.

questions.q: If a univariate extreme event is defined as one that occurs on average once every 50 observations, and two variables are independent, how often would a joint extreme event (both variables extreme simultaneously) be expected to occur?
choices: Once every 50 observations
Once every 100 observations
Once every 2,500 observations
Once every 10,000 observations
questions.explanation: For independent variables, joint extreme-event probabilities multiply: 1/50 × 1/50 = 1/2,500, so a joint extreme event is expected once every 2,500 observations.
